title: Legal & Ethical — LPS Act & Patient Rights (Set 1)
subtitle: 5150/5250 holds, consent, right to refuse, confidentiality, restraint law & mandated reporting
scenario: California LPS Act, patient rights, consent, confidentiality, restraint/seclusion law, and the psychiatric technician's legal and ethical responsibilities.

steps.q: Under California's LPS Act, a person may be placed on a 5150 hold for up to 72 hours when they are a danger to others, gravely disabled, or:

opts.rat: The three criteria for a 5150 hold are danger to self, danger to others, or grave disability due to a mental disorder.
Insurance status has no bearing on an involuntary hold.
Refusing an activity is not grounds for involuntary detention.
Wanting to leave, by itself, is not a criterion for a hold.

steps.q: A 5250 certification under the LPS Act authorizes involuntary treatment for up to:

opts.rat: 24 hours does not correspond to any LPS certification period.
A 5250 provides up to 14 additional days of intensive treatment and requires a certification review hearing.
72 hours is the 5150 hold, not the 5250 certification.
Six months relates to conservatorship-level care, not a 5250.

steps.q: 'Gravely disabled' under the LPS Act means the person is unable, due to a mental disorder, to provide for their basic needs of:

opts.rat: These are not the statutory basic needs.
These are not the defined basic-needs criteria.
Grave disability is the inability to provide for one's basic personal needs for food, clothing, or shelter because of a mental disorder.
This is unrelated to the legal standard.

steps.q: A voluntarily admitted client states she wants to leave the unit. The technician should:

opts.rat: Detaining a voluntary client without a legal hold can constitute false imprisonment.

opts.text: Tell her she can never leave
opts.rat: This is false and violates her rights.
Ignoring a discharge request is inappropriate and disrespects the client's rights.

opts.text: Notify the nurse so the request can be addressed per policy, as a voluntary client may request discharge
opts.rat: Voluntary clients retain the right to request discharge; the team follows policy (which may include a discharge process or evaluation).

steps.q: An involuntarily held client who has decisional capacity refuses his antipsychotic medication. In the absence of an emergency or court order, the client:

opts.text: Generally retains the right to refuse the medication
opts.rat: In California, a held client with capacity retains the right to refuse antipsychotics unless there is an emergency or a court (capacity) determination.

opts.text: Must be medicated against his will immediately
opts.rat: Involuntary hold alone does not authorize forced non-emergency medication.
A hold does not strip a client of all rights.
Refusal does not mandate discharge.

steps.q: For informed consent to be valid, the client must receive information about the treatment, its risks and benefits, alternatives, and must:

opts.text: Agree only after being told there are no risks
opts.rat: Withholding risks invalidates consent.

opts.text: Give consent voluntarily and have the capacity to understand
opts.rat: Valid informed consent requires adequate information, voluntariness, and the capacity to understand and decide.
Coerced consent is not valid; it must be voluntary.
Consent requires disclosure of risks and alternatives, not benefits alone.

steps.q: A visitor asks the technician whether a specific client is on the unit and what their diagnosis is. The technician should:

opts.text: Confirm the admission and share the diagnosis
opts.rat: Disclosing admission or diagnosis without authorization violates confidentiality.

opts.text: Give the information because the visitor seems to be family
opts.rat: Appearances do not authorize disclosure of protected information.

opts.text: Not confirm or deny the client's presence and refer the visitor to the nurse/policy
opts.rat: Confidentiality requires protecting even the fact of admission; disclosures follow authorization and policy.

opts.text: Post the client's information at the nurses' station
opts.rat: Publicly displaying protected information breaches confidentiality.

steps.q: A client tells the technician of a specific, credible plan to seriously harm a named individual. The legal duty to protect that identifiable person derives from:

opts.rat: Confidentiality yields to the duty to protect a threatened third party.

opts.text: The client's right to refuse treatment
opts.rat: This is unrelated to the duty to protect.
Normalization is a developmental-disability concept, not a legal duty here.
Tarasoff established a duty to protect identifiable potential victims from a client's serious threat; the team must be notified so appropriate steps are taken.

steps.q: Restraints or seclusion in a psychiatric setting may be used:

opts.text: Only for imminent safety risk, as a last resort, with an order and monitoring
opts.rat: Restraint/seclusion is a last-resort safety measure requiring a time-limited order, continuous monitoring, and documentation.
Restraint/seclusion may never be used for staff convenience.
Using restraint as punishment is prohibited.

opts.text: Whenever a client is verbally rude
opts.rat: Rudeness does not justify restraint; less restrictive measures apply.

steps.q: Administering an injection to a competent client who has clearly refused it, in a non-emergency, may legally constitute:

opts.rat: Intentional unconsented contact is battery, not merely negligence.
Nonconsensual touching/treatment of a competent, refusing client can constitute battery.
Defamation involves false statements harming reputation, not physical contact.
This is unrelated to the legal concept.

steps.q: Under California patient-rights law, a client's right to keep personal belongings or have visitors can be denied only when:

opts.text: The client is on any involuntary hold
opts.rat: A hold alone does not automatically deny these rights.
Convenience is not good cause.

opts.text: There is good cause, it is documented, and it is reviewed
opts.rat: Enumerated patient rights may be denied only for good cause that is documented and subject to review.
Refusing medication is not grounds to deny personal-rights protections.

steps.q: A psychiatric technician who suspects that a dependent adult client has been physically abused is legally required to:

opts.text: Keep it private unless proof is obtained
opts.rat: Mandated reporters act on reasonable suspicion, not proof.

opts.text: Ask the family to handle it internally
opts.rat: The reporting duty is personal and cannot be delegated to family.
Reporting must be timely, not delayed.

opts.text: Report the suspicion to the appropriate authority as a mandated reporter
opts.rat: Psychiatric technicians are mandated reporters and must promptly report suspected abuse of a dependent adult.

steps.q: Detaining a client who has no legal hold and is free to leave, against their will, may constitute:

opts.rat: Unlawfully confining a client who has the right to leave can constitute false imprisonment.
Assault is creating apprehension of harmful contact, not confinement.
Slander is spoken defamation, unrelated to confinement.
This legal concept does not apply to the situation.

steps.q: The principle of the 'least restrictive environment' means that the technician should:

opts.text: Use restraints early to prevent any risk
opts.rat: This is the opposite of least-restrictive care.

opts.text: Use the least restrictive intervention that maintains safety before more restrictive ones
opts.rat: Care must use the least restrictive effective option, escalating only when necessary for safety.
Routine seclusion is highly restrictive and inappropriate.

opts.text: Keep all clients in locked rooms
opts.rat: This violates least-restrictive principles and patient rights.

steps.q: When documenting care, the psychiatric technician should record entries that are:

opts.text: Based on personal opinions and assumptions
opts.rat: Opinions and assumptions are not appropriate for the legal record.

opts.text: Written in pencil so they can be changed
opts.rat: Records must be permanent; altering them improperly is illegal.
The medical record is a legal document; entries must be objective, accurate, timely, and factual.
Documentation should be timely to be accurate and legally sound.

steps.q: A client on a 72-hour hold has the right to be informed of:

opts.rat: Clients must be informed of their legal status and rights.
Rights information is broader than a discharge date.
This omits the required rights disclosure.

opts.text: The reason for the hold and their legal rights, including access to a patients' rights advocate
opts.rat: Clients on a hold must be advised of the reason and their rights, including access to a rights advocate.

steps.q: A psychiatric technician is asked to perform a task outside the legal scope of practice for their license. The technician should:

opts.text: Decline and notify the nurse/supervisor, staying within the legal scope of practice
opts.rat: The technician must practice only within the legal scope and communicate the concern to the supervisor.
Acting outside one's scope endangers clients and risks license discipline.
Delegating an out-of-scope task does not make it lawful.

opts.text: Perform it and document that they were told to
opts.rat: Following an order does not authorize practicing beyond one's legal scope.

steps.q: Confidential client information may be appropriately shared:

opts.rat: Disclosing to one's own family breaches confidentiality.

opts.text: With members of the treatment team on a need-to-know basis for care
opts.rat: Sharing within the treatment team for the client's care, on a need-to-know basis, is permitted and appropriate.
Posting client information anywhere publicly is a serious violation.

opts.text: With curious coworkers not involved in the client's care
opts.rat: Access is limited to those involved in the client's care.

steps.q: An LPS conservatorship may be established for a person who, because of a mental disorder, is:

opts.rat: Transient sadness does not meet the legal standard.
Disagreement is not grounds for conservatorship.

opts.text: Gravely disabled and unable to provide for basic personal needs
opts.rat: LPS conservatorship applies to persons found gravely disabled due to a mental disorder, unable to meet basic personal needs.
Seeking a second opinion is a right, not grounds for conservatorship.

steps.q: A technician observes a coworker diverting a client's medication. The MOST appropriate action is to:

opts.rat: Ignoring diversion endangers clients and violates professional/legal duties.

opts.text: Confront the coworker privately and keep it between them
opts.rat: This does not fulfill the duty to report and protect clients.

opts.text: Take the medication to test it
opts.rat: Handling the medication this way is improper and outside protocol.

opts.text: Report the observation through the proper channels (supervisor/facility policy)
opts.rat: Suspected medication diversion must be reported through proper channels to protect clients and meet legal/ethical obligations.
